devanagari: उद्धव उवाच । ततः स आगत्य पुरं स्वपित्रोः । चिकीर्षया शं बलदेवसंयुतः । निपात्य तुङ्गाद् रिपुयूथनाथं । हतं व्यकर्षद् व्यसुमोजसोर्व्याम् ॥ १...
english_devnagari: uddhava uvācatataḥ sa āgatya puraṃ svapitroḥcikīrṣayā śaṃ baladevasaṃyutaḥnipātya tuṅgād ripuyūthanāthaṃhataṃ vyakarṣad vyasumojasorvyām
translation:
Uddhava said:
Then, with a desire to make his parents happy, he (Kṛṣṇa) accompanied by Balarāma came to the city (Mathurā) and forcibly pulled down Kaṃsa, the head of the enemy-force, from his royal seat, and after killing him, dragged him along the ground (though dead).

devanagari: सान्दीपनेः सकृत् प्रोक्तं ब्रह्माधीत्य सविस्तरम् । तस्मै प्रादाद् वरं पुत्रं मृतं पञ्चजनोदरात् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: sāndīpaneḥ sakṛt proktaṃ brahmādhītya savistaramtasmai prādād varaṃ putraṃ mṛtaṃ pañcajanodarāt
translation: He learnt from Sāndīpani[1] the Veda with all its details (the six accessories of the Veda) which was recited to him but once. He restored his dead son to him (Sāndīpani) from the belly of Pañcajana[2].
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Sāndīpani—Teacher of Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma (DHM. 279).

Footnote [2]:
Pañcajana—Name of a demon who assumed the form of a conch-shell and was slain by Kṛṣṇa to recover Sāndīpani’s son. But as he was not found in Pañcajana’s belly, Kṛṣṇa brought him back from Yama. For details—infra X Ch. 45.

devanagari: समाहुता भीष्मककन्यया ये । श्रियः सवर्णेन बुभूषयैषाम् । गान्धर्ववृत्त्या मिषतां स्वभागं । जह्रे पदं मूर्ध्नि दधत्सुपर्णः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: samāhutā bhīṣmakakanyayā yeśriyaḥ savarṇena bubhūṣayaiṣāmgāndharvavṛttyā miṣatāṃ svabhāgaṃjahre padaṃ mūrdhni dadhatsuparṇaḥ
translation: Just as Garuḍa snatched away his share (of nectar), Kṛṣṇa, in the very presence of kings invited[3] on behalf of the Princess of Bhīṣmaka, carried away Rukmiṇī—his own share (as she was an incarnation of Lakṣmī)—with a view to marry her by the Gāndharva form of marriage,[4] thus setting his foot on the heads of the kings[5].
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā accepts the v.l. samāhṛta i.e. who were attracted by her (Rukmiṇī’s) beauty etc.

Footnote [4]:
Modern ‘love-marriage’. So called due to its supposed prevalence among the Gandharvas. ASD. 398 is wrong when it states that it is performed ‘without ceremonies’. Smṛti Candrikā and other digests state that homa and saptapadī are necessary. For details Kane History of Dharma Śāstra Vol. II—i. Ch. IX. P. 521-24.

Footnote [5]:
Kings who were invited by her brother Rukmi whose name had two syllables common with the name ‘Rukmiṇī’—(Bhāvāratha Dīpikā).

devanagari: ककुद्मिनोऽविद्धनसो दमित्वा । स्वयंवरे नाग्नजितीमुवाह । तद्‍भग्नमानानपि गृध्यतोऽज्ञान् । जघ्नेऽक्षतः शस्त्रभृतः स्वशस्त्रैः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: kakudmino'viddhanaso damitvāsvayaṃvare nāgnajitīmuvāhatad‍bhagnamānānapi gṛdhyato'jñānjaghne'kṣataḥ śastrabhṛtaḥ svaśastraiḥ
translation: In another svayaṃvara, he tamed the bulls whose noses had not been bored and married the Princess (Satyabhāmā) of Nagnajit. He discomfited the foolish kings who, despite their humiliation (by Kṛṣṇa’s fulfilment of the marriage condition) still entertained a desire for her and came to fight him with their weapons, though he could not be injured by them.

devanagari: प्रियं प्रभुर्ग्राम्य इव प्रियाया । विधित्सुरार्च्छद् द्युतरुं यदर्थे । वज्र्याद्रवत्तं सगणो रुषान्धः । क्रीडामृगो नूनमयं वधूनाम् ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: priyaṃ prabhurgrāmya iva priyāyāvidhitsurārcchad dyutaruṃ yadarthevajryādravattaṃ sagaṇo ruṣāndhaḥkrīḍāmṛgo nūnamayaṃ vadhūnām
translation: Like an ordinary man (under the thumb of his wife) the Lord took away the celestial tree Pārijāta to grant the desire of his beloved (Satyabhāmā). At this, Indra (who was incited by Śacī) like a pet deer of women, got blind with rage and pursued him with his army (of gods)[6].
purport:
Footnote [6]:
Indra’s ingratitude is obvious, as it was he who invited Kṛṣṇa to get back the earrings of his mother Aditi from Bhaumāsura. Kṛṣṇa went to Svarga with Satyabhāmā and restored the ear-rings (Vide infra X. Gh. 59).

devanagari: सुतं मृधे खं वपुषा ग्रसन्तं । दृष्ट्वा सुनाभोन्मथितं धरित्र्या । आमंत्रितस्तत् तनयाय शेषं । दत्त्वा तदन्तःपुरमाविवेश ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: sutaṃ mṛdhe khaṃ vapuṣā grasantaṃdṛṣṭvā sunābhonmathitaṃ dharitryāāmaṃtritastat tanayāya śeṣaṃdattvā tadantaḥpuramāviveśa
translation: Seeing her son (Naraka) who was (as if) swallowing the sky with his body, cut down by the Sudarśana disc, he (Kṛṣṇa) was prayed by the goddess of the Earth (Naraka’s mother). Having given the remaining (unannexed) kingdom to Naraka’s son, Kṛṣṇa entered the harem.[7]
purport:
Footnote [7]:
For details vide infra X. 59.

devanagari: तत्राहृतास्ता नरदेवकन्याः । कुजेन दृष्ट्वा हरिमार्तबन्धुम् । उत्थाय सद्यो जगृहुः प्रहर्ष । व्रीडानुरागप्रहितावलोकैः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: tatrāhṛtāstā naradevakanyāḥkujena dṛṣṭvā harimārtabandhumutthāya sadyo jagṛhuḥ praharṣavrīḍānurāgaprahitāvalokaiḥ
translation: Seeing Hari, the friend of the afflicted person, the princesses who were abducted by Naraka, immediately rose to receive him with their glances full of rapture, bashfulness and love.

devanagari: आसां मुहूर्त एकस्मिन् नानागारेषु योषिताम् । सविधं जगृहे पाणीन् अनुरूपः स्वमायया ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: āsāṃ muhūrta ekasmin nānāgāreṣu yoṣitāmsavidhaṃ jagṛhe pāṇīn anurūpaḥ svamāyayā
translation: Assuming suitable forms by his māyā powers, he simultaneously married them all, with proper formalities, in different apartments.

devanagari: तास्वपत्यान्यजनयद् आत्मतुल्यानि सर्वतः । एकैकस्यां दश दश प्रकृतेर्विबुभूषया ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: tāsvapatyānyajanayad ātmatulyāni sarvataḥekaikasyāṃ daśa daśa prakṛtervibubhūṣayā
translation: With a desire to expand his prakṛti (or to become many for the sake of prakṛti), he got from each of them ten children equal to him in all respects.

devanagari: कालमागधशाल्वादीन् अनीकै रुन्धतः पुरम् । अजीघनत् स्वयं दिव्यं स्वपुंसां तेज आदिशत् ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: kālamāgadhaśālvādīn anīkai rundhataḥ puramajīghanat svayaṃ divyaṃ svapuṃsāṃ teja ādiśat
translation: He commissioned his great prowess and glory in his men (devotees like Mucukunda, Bhīma), and through them he got killed Kālayavana, Jarāsandha, Śālva and others who, with their armies, laid siege to his town.

devanagari: शम्बरं द्विविदं बाणं मुरं बल्वलमेव च । अन्यांश्च दन्तवक्रादीन् अवधीत्कांश्च घातयत् ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: śambaraṃ dvividaṃ bāṇaṃ muraṃ balvalameva caanyāṃśca dantavakrādīn avadhītkāṃśca ghātayat
translation: He caused the death of some like Śambara[8], Dvivida[9], Bāṇa[10], Mura[11] and Balvala[12] (through others), while he killed others like Dantavaktra[13] (personally).
purport:
Footnote [8]:
Śambara: A demon who stole away Kṛṣṇa’s son Pradyumna soon after his birth, and threw him into the sea. Providentially Pradyumnawas saved and ḥe killed Śambara—Bhāratīya Paurāṇika Kośa 197.

Footnote [9]:
Dvivida—A monkey friend of Naraka; to avenge Naraka’s death he went on destroying the towns in Kṛṣna’s kingdom. At Raivata hill, he offended Balarāma insulting his fair companions. In the ensuing fight Balarāma killed him—Purāṇa Index. 2.150.

Footnote [10]:
Bāṇa—Eldest son of Bali; a devotee of Śiva; arrested Kṛṣṇa’s grandson Aniruddha who courted his daughter Uṣā. In the battle that followed for the rescue of Aniruddha, god Śiva and Skanda assisted Bāṇa but were finally overpowered by Kṛṣṇa. At Śiva’s request Kṛṣṇa spared Baṇa’s [Bāṇa’s?] life and returned with Uṣā and Aniruddha—DHM. 42.

Footnote [11]:
Mura—A demon ally of Naraka; defended Naraka’s capital Prāgjyotiṣa with his seven thousand sons. But he along with his sons was killed by Kṛṣṇa with his disc Sudarśana—DHM. 212.

Footnote [12]:
Balvala—A demon who ruined the sacrifices at Naimiṣa; was killed by Balarāma at the request of the sages.

Footnote [13]:
Dantavaktra—King of Karuṣa; took side against Kṛṣṇa on behalf of Jarāsandha, Śālva and was killed by Kṛṣṇa—DHM. 80.

devanagari: अथ ते भ्रातृपुत्राणां पक्षयोः पतितान् नृपान् । चचाल भूः कुरुक्षेत्रं येषां आपततां बलैः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: atha te bhrātṛputrāṇāṃ pakṣayoḥ patitān nṛpāncacāla bhūḥ kurukṣetraṃ yeṣāṃ āpatatāṃ balaiḥ
translation: He then caused the destruction of kings who espoused the cause of your brothers’ sons (Kauravas and Pāṇḍavas). By the marching of their armies to Kurukṣetra, the earth was as if made to tremble.

devanagari: स कर्णदुःशासनसौबलानां । कुमंत्रपाकेन हतश्रियायुषम् । सुयोधनं सानुचरं शयानं । भग्नोरुमूर्व्यां न ननन्द पश्यन् ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: sa karṇaduḥśāsanasaubalānāṃkumaṃtrapākena hataśriyāyuṣamsuyodhanaṃ sānucaraṃ śayānaṃbhagnorumūrvyāṃ na nananda paśyan
translation: He was not satisfied when he saw Suyodhana, along with his followers, lying prostrate on the ground with thighs broken and with his royal splendour and life cut short by the evil advice tendered by Karṇa, Duḥśāsana and Śakuni.

devanagari: कियान् भुवोऽयं क्षपितोरुभारो । यद्द्रोणभीष्मार्जुन भीममूलैः । अष्टादशाक्षौहिणिको मदंशैः । आस्ते बलं दुर्विषहं यदूनाम् ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: kiyān bhuvo'yaṃ kṣapitorubhāroyaddroṇabhīṣmārjuna bhīmamūlaiḥaṣṭādaśākṣauhiṇiko madaṃśaiḥāste balaṃ durviṣahaṃ yadūnām
translation: (He thought to himself): What an insignificant portion of the heavy burden of the earth is relieved, though an army of eighteen Akṣauhiṇīs is annihilated through (the medium of) Bhīṣma, Droṇa, Arjuna and Bhīma! For there exists an irresistible army of Yādavas headed by (Pradyumna and others who are) my parts[14] (aṃśas).
purport:
Footnote [14]:
Padaratnāvalī construes it with Bhīṣma, Droṇa etc.

devanagari: मिथो यदैषां भविता विवादो । मध्वामदाताम्रविलोचनानाम् । नैषां वधोपाय इयानतोऽन्यो । मय्युद्यतेऽन्तर्दधते स्वयं स्म ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: mitho yadaiṣāṃ bhavitā vivādomadhvāmadātāmravilocanānāmnaiṣāṃ vadhopāya iyānato'nyomayyudyate'ntardadhate svayaṃ sma
translation: There is no other strategem to kill them (Yādavas) except when there will be a quarrel among themselves, with their eyes reddish with intoxication. When I prepare to leave this world, they will automatically disappear.

devanagari: एवं सञ्चिन्त्य भगवान् स्वराज्ये स्थाप्य धर्मजम् । नन्दयामास सुहृदः साधूनां वर्त्म दर्शयन् ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ sañcintya bhagavān svarājye sthāpya dharmajamnandayāmāsa suhṛdaḥ sādhūnāṃ vartma darśayan
translation: After planning thus, the Lord, establishing the son of Dharma (Yudhiṣṭhira) in his own kingdom, gave delight to his friends and showed thereby the path of the righteous people.

devanagari: उत्तरायां धृतः पूरोः वंशः साध्वभिमन्युना । स वै द्रौण्यस्त्रसंछिन्नः पुनर्भगवता धृतः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: uttarāyāṃ dhṛtaḥ pūroḥ vaṃśaḥ sādhvabhimanyunāsa vai drauṇyastrasaṃchinnaḥ punarbhagavatā dhṛtaḥ
translation: The continuity of the Puru family, which was properly secured by Abhimanyu in Uttarā, but which was verily destroyed by the missiles of Aśvatthāman, was again restored and protected by the Lord.

devanagari: अयाजयद् धर्मसुतं अश्वमेधैस्त्रिभिर्विभुः । सोऽपि क्ष्मामनुजै रक्षन् रेमे कृष्णमनुव्रतः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: ayājayad dharmasutaṃ aśvamedhaistribhirvibhuḥso'pi kṣmāmanujai rakṣan reme kṛṣṇamanuvrataḥ
translation: The Omnipresent Lord caused the son of Dharma (Yudhiṣṭhira) to perform the horse-sacrifice thrice. He (Yudhiṣṭhira) who was devoted to Kṛṣṇa, enjoyed himself protecting the earth with the help of his younger brothers.

devanagari: भगवान् अपि विश्वात्मा लोकवेदपथानुगः । कामान् सिषेवे द्वार्वत्यां असक्तः साङ्ख्यमास्थितः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: bhagavān api viśvātmā lokavedapathānugaḥkāmān siṣeve dvārvatyāṃ asaktaḥ sāṅkhyamāsthitaḥ
translation: The glorious Lord also, the Soul of the universe and a follower of the path prescribed in Śrutis and Smṛtis enjoyed all worldly pleasures at Dvārakā. But as he was firmly fixed in the Sāṅkhya Philosophy, he remained non-attached (due to his realization of the distinction between Prakṛti and Puruṣa).

devanagari: स्निग्धस्मितावलोकेन वाचा पीयूषकल्पया । चरित्रेणानवद्येन श्रीनिकेतेन चात्मना ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: snigdhasmitāvalokena vācā pīyūṣakalpayācaritreṇānavadyena śrīniketena cātmanā
translation: With his lovely smiling looks and words sweet as nectar, with spotless character and with his body as an abode of Śrī (Goddess Lakṣmī) he gave delight to this world as well as to the next, especially so to the Yadus. At night, he showed momentary friendship to women who obtained the pleasure of his companionship.

devanagari: इमं लोकममुं चैव रमयन् सुतरां यदून् । रेमे क्षणदया दत्त क्षणस्त्रीक्षणसौहृदः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: imaṃ lokamamuṃ caiva ramayan sutarāṃ yadūnreme kṣaṇadayā datta kṣaṇastrīkṣaṇasauhṛdaḥ
translation: With his lovely smiling looks and words sweet as nectar, with spotless character and with his body as an abode of Śrī (Goddess Lakṣmī) he gave delight to this world as well as to the next, especially so to the Yadus. At night, he showed momentary friendship to women who obtained the pleasure of his companionship.

devanagari: तस्यैवं रममाणस्य संवत्सरगणान् बहून् । गृहमेधेषु योगेषु विरागः समजायत ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: tasyaivaṃ ramamāṇasya saṃvatsaragaṇān bahūngṛhamedheṣu yogeṣu virāgaḥ samajāyata
translation: While he was enjoying himself thus for pretty long years, He became unattached to the householder’s life and the pleasures therein.

devanagari: दैवाधीनेषु कामेषु दैवाधीनः स्वयं पुमान् । को विश्रम्भेत योगेन योगेश्वरमनुव्रतः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: daivādhīneṣu kāmeṣu daivādhīnaḥ svayaṃ pumānko viśrambheta yogena yogeśvaramanuvrataḥ
translation: [15] The objects of enjoyment are in the power of the Fate. Man himself is at the mercy of the Fate. What person devoted to the Lord of Yoga (Kṛṣṇa) by yogic process, will put faith in these (objects etc.)?
purport:
Footnote [15]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā differs: When the Lord himself who had everything in his power, felt non-attachment, who would love or entertain faith in things in the power of the fate? A person devoted to Lord Kṛṣṇa would never do so.

devanagari: पुर्यां कदाचित् क्रीडद्भिः यदुभोजकुमारकैः । कोपिता मुनयः शेपुः भगवन् मतकोविदाः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: puryāṃ kadācit krīḍadbhiḥ yadubhojakumārakaiḥkopitā munayaḥ śepuḥ bhagavan matakovidāḥ
translation: Once upon a time, in the city of Dvārakā (some) sages were offended by Yādava and Bhoja lads while at play. The sages who knew the intention of the Lord, cursed them.

devanagari: ततः कतिपयैर्मासैः वृष्णिभोज अन्धकादयः । ययुः प्रभासं संहृष्टा रथैर्देवविमोहिताः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: tataḥ katipayairmāsaiḥ vṛṣṇibhoja andhakādayaḥyayuḥ prabhāsaṃ saṃhṛṣṭā rathairdevavimohitāḥ
translation: Then after some months, Vṛṣṇis, Bhojas, Andhakas and others, being deluded by God, rode in their chariots to Prabhāsa, in great delight.

devanagari: तत्र स्नात्वा पितॄन् देवान् ऋषींश्चैव तदम्भसा । तर्पयित्वाथ विप्रेभ्यो गावो बहुगुणा ददुः ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: tatra snātvā pitṝn devān ṛṣīṃścaiva tadambhasātarpayitvātha viprebhyo gāvo bahuguṇā daduḥ
translation: Thereafter, performing ablutions and offering libations to Manes, gods and sages they, with that water, gave cows of many good qualities, to Brāhmaṇas.

devanagari: हिरण्यं रजतं शय्यां वासांस्यजिनकम्बलान् । यानं रथानिभान् कन्या धरां वृत्तिकरीमपि ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: hiraṇyaṃ rajataṃ śayyāṃ vāsāṃsyajinakambalānyānaṃ rathānibhān kanyā dharāṃ vṛttikarīmapi
translation: They donated to Brāhmaṇas gold and silver, clothes and beds, skins and woollen blankets, horses and chariots, elephants, girls and land sufficient for maintenance, sumptuous food with a view to pleasing the Lord. Those brave warriors whose life was dedicated to the service of cows and Brāhmaṇas bowed to them (Brāhmaṇas) with their heads touching the ground.

devanagari: अन्नं चोरुरसं तेभ्यो दत्त्वा भगवदर्पणम् । गोविप्रार्थासवः शूराः प्रणेमुर्भुवि मूर्धभिः ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: annaṃ corurasaṃ tebhyo dattvā bhagavadarpaṇamgoviprārthāsavaḥ śūrāḥ praṇemurbhuvi mūrdhabhiḥ
translation:
They donated to Brāhmaṇas gold and silver, clothes and beds, skins and woollen blankets, horses and chariots, elephants, girls and land sufficient for maintenance, sumptuous food with a view to pleasing the Lord. Those brave warriors whose life was dedicated to the service of cows and Brāhmaṇas bowed to them (Brāhmaṇas) with their heads touching the ground.
